name: Okinawa Prefecture (沖縄県 / 琉球 Ryukyu)
summary: Okinawa Prefecture (沖縄県 Okinawa-ken in Japanese, also encompassing the Ryukyu 琉球 cultural identity) is a Southwestern Japanese civil-law Japanese prefecture comprising the Ryukyu Archipelago — structurally distinctive globally as the only Japanese prefecture with a continuous independent-kingdom framework predating Japanese incorporation (Ryukyu Kingdom 1429-1879 under the Sho Dynasty — independent maritime trading kingdom with tributary relationships with both Ming/Qing China and Tokugawa Japan via Satsuma Domain 1609-1879, distinct from rest-of-Japan's pre-Meiji feudal-domain framework), as the only Japanese prefecture subject to extended post-WWII US military administration (US Civil Administration of the Ryukyu Islands 1945-1972 — 27-year US administration following Battle of Okinawa April-June 1945 with ~150,000 civilian deaths, reverted to Japan 15 May 1972 under the Okinawa Reversion Agreement 1971), as the central jurisdiction for the US-Japan Status of Forces Agreement (SOFA) framework concentration (~70% of all US military base footprint in Japan concentrated in Okinawa Prefecture despite Okinawa comprising only ~0.6% of Japanese land area — Kadena Air Base + Marine Corps Air Station Futenma + Camp Schwab + Henoko relocation framework), and as a distinctive jurisdiction within Japan for Ryukyuan-cultural-identity-vs-Japanese-cultural-assimilation tension (Ryukyuan languages 沖縄口 Uchinaaguchi recognised by UNESCO as endangered languages 2009 — distinct from Japanese language but historically suppressed under Meiji-era assimilation policies 1879-1945). Okinawa was annexed by Meiji Japan via the Ryukyu Disposition framework 1872-1879 (abolishing the Ryukyu Kingdom and establishing Okinawa Prefecture 27 March 1879), suffered the Battle of Okinawa April-June 1945 (deadliest WWII Pacific battle on Japanese soil), was administered by the US Civil Administration of the Ryukyu Islands 1950-1972, and reverted to Japan on 15 May 1972. Okinawa Prefecture comprises ~2,281 km² (160+ islands across Ryukyu Archipelago) with a population of ~1.47 million. Family-law framework operates under the federal Japanese civil-law framework (Japanese Civil Code 1898 Family Law portion + Family Law Reform Act 2024 reintroducing joint parental authority post-divorce effective 2026) applied uniformly across Japanese prefectures. Parental authority (親権 shinken) and child custody operate under Japanese Civil Code framework — historically sole-custody framework until 2024 reform. The Naha District Court is the primary trial court; the Fukuoka High Court Naha Branch is the apex appellate court; final appellate jurisdiction lies with the Supreme Court of Japan. Okinawa is silent on 'parental alienation' as a statutory label. Japan is a Hague Convention 1980 signatory and party (acceded 24 January 2014 effective 1 April 2014).

statutory_framework.citation: Japanese Civil Code 1898 Family Law portion (民法 Minpo Articles 725-881)
statutory_framework.title: Japanese Civil Code Family Law
statutory_framework.relevance: Japanese Federal Civil Code 1898 Family Law portion applicable uniformly across Okinawa Prefecture.

statutory_framework.citation: Japanese Family Law Reform Act 2024 (joint parental authority post-divorce)
statutory_framework.title: Japanese Family Law Reform Act 2024
statutory_framework.relevance: Japanese Federal Act of 17 May 2024 reintroducing joint parental authority post-divorce — effective 2026 — applicable uniformly across Okinawa Prefecture.

statutory_framework.title: Okinawa Reversion Agreement
statutory_framework.relevance: US-Japan bilateral agreement of 17 June 1971 effective 15 May 1972 reverting administration of Ryukyu Islands from US Civil Administration to Japan.

statutory_framework.title: US-Japan SOFA
statutory_framework.relevance: US-Japan bilateral Status of Forces Agreement of 19 January 1960 applicable to US military bases in Okinawa post-1972 reversion — substantial Okinawa-prefecture-level political contestation.

statutory_framework.title: Hague Convention 1980 — Japan
statutory_framework.relevance: Japanese Hague Convention 1980 accession 24 January 2014 effective 1 April 2014 — applicable uniformly across Okinawa Prefecture.

apex_courts.name: Fukuoka High Court Naha Branch
apex_courts.role: Apex domestic appellate court for civil and criminal matters from Okinawa Prefecture.

apex_courts.name: Supreme Court of Japan
apex_courts.role: Final appellate court for civil and criminal matters from Okinawa Prefecture.

professional_regulators.name: Japanese Ministry of Health, Labour and Welfare (厚生労働省)
professional_regulators.role: Japanese Federal professional regulatory framework applicable in Okinawa Prefecture.

anonymisation_convention: Okinawa family-court decisions are anonymised per Japanese court practice using initials.

key_developments.title: Ryukyu Kingdom unified
key_developments.description: Ryukyu Kingdom unified 1429 under King Sho Hashi — beginning of Sho Dynasty independent maritime trading kingdom framework 1429-1879.

key_developments.title: Satsuma invasion
key_developments.description: Satsuma Domain invasion of Ryukyu Kingdom 1609 — beginning of dual-tributary framework (Ming/Qing China + Tokugawa Japan via Satsuma) 1609-1879.

key_developments.title: Ryukyu Disposition + Okinawa Prefecture established
key_developments.description: Meiji Japan Ryukyu Disposition framework 1872-1879 abolishing Ryukyu Kingdom — Okinawa Prefecture established 27 March 1879.

key_developments.title: Battle of Okinawa
key_developments.description: Battle of Okinawa April-June 1945 — deadliest WWII Pacific battle on Japanese soil with ~150,000 civilian deaths + US military occupation following 23 June 1945 surrender.

key_developments.title: US Civil Administration of the Ryukyu Islands
key_developments.description: US Civil Administration of the Ryukyu Islands established 1950 — 22-year US administration 1950-1972.

key_developments.title: Okinawa Reversion to Japan
key_developments.description: Okinawa Reversion to Japan effective 15 May 1972 under Okinawa Reversion Agreement 1971.

key_developments.title: UNESCO endangered-language recognition
key_developments.description: UNESCO Atlas of the World's Languages in Danger 2009 recognising Ryukyuan languages 沖縄口 as endangered.

key_developments.title: Japan Hague Convention 1980 accession
key_developments.description: Japanese Hague Convention 1980 accession 24 January 2014 effective 1 April 2014 — applicable uniformly across Okinawa Prefecture.

key_developments.title: Joint parental authority reform
key_developments.description: Japanese Family Law Reform Act 2024 reintroducing joint parental authority post-divorce effective 2026 — applicable uniformly across Okinawa Prefecture.

structural_findings: Okinawa operates a federal Japanese civil-law prefecture framework — places Okinawa in the Southwestern Japanese Ryukyu-Archipelago cluster.

Only Japanese prefecture with continuous independent-kingdom framework predating Japanese incorporation (Ryukyu Kingdom 1429-1879) is structurally distinctive globally — distinct from rest-of-Japan's pre-Meiji feudal-domain framework.
Only Japanese prefecture subject to extended post-WWII US military administration (1945-1972 27-year US administration) is structurally distinctive globally.
Central jurisdiction for US-Japan SOFA framework concentration (~70% of US military base footprint in Japan on ~0.6% of Japanese land area) is structurally distinctive globally.
Ryukyuan-cultural-identity-vs-Japanese-cultural-assimilation tension is structurally distinctive within Japanese prefecture cluster — UNESCO-recognised endangered Ryukyuan languages 2009 framework.
Battle of Okinawa April-June 1945 framework is structurally distinctive globally — deadliest WWII Pacific battle on Japanese soil.
1609-1879 dual-tributary Ming/Qing-China-and-Tokugawa-Japan-via-Satsuma framework is structurally distinctive globally — only modern East-Asian jurisdiction with dual-tributary historical framework.
Japanese Hague-Convention-1980-accession-2014 status applies — late accession framework.
editorial_notes: Okinawa Prefecture jurisdiction sidecar — civil-law Southwestern Japanese Ryukyu-Archipelago Japanese-prefecture (Japanese Civil Code 1898 + Japanese Family Law Reform Act 2024 + Okinawa Reversion Agreement 1971/1972 + US-Japan SOFA 1960 + Ryukyu Kingdom 1429-1879 + Satsuma invasion 1609 + Ryukyu Disposition 1879 + Battle of Okinawa 1945 + US Civil Administration 1950-1972 + UNESCO endangered Ryukyuan languages 2009 + Japan Hague-1980-2014). Only Japanese prefecture with continuous independent-kingdom framework predating Japanese incorporation globally + only Japanese prefecture subject to extended post-WWII US military administration + central jurisdiction for US-Japan SOFA framework concentration + Ryukyuan-cultural-identity-vs-Japanese-cultural-assimilation tension + Battle of Okinawa 1945 framework + 1609-1879 dual-tributary framework.
PA-recognition: silent statutory + no-apex-position + silent regulator.

Joins Southwestern-Japanese + civil-law + Japanese-prefecture cluster + Ryukyu-Kingdom-1429-1879-independent-kingdom-globally-distinctive + US-Civil-Administration-1945-1972 + Okinawa-Reversion-1972 + US-Japan-SOFA-base-concentration + Ryukyuan-languages-UNESCO-endangered + Satsuma-1609-dual-tributary + Battle-of-Okinawa-1945 + Japan-Hague-1980-2014 clusters within the corpus.
